tagged_story_for_tts:
"Expertise under pressure isn’t skill—it’s a hostage negotiation with your own brain.

The myth? "Elites perform better when the stakes rise." The truth? Cognitive bandwidth collapses under threat. A 2018 Nature study found surgeons’ error rates spiked 47% in high-stress ORs—not because they lacked skill, but because pressure hijacks prefrontal cortex function, defaulting to rigid, outdated scripts. The same pattern doomed NASA’s Challenger engineers: they knew the O-rings would fail in cold, but hierarchical pressure turned expertise into compliance. Even chess grandmasters, per a PNAS analysis, blunder 3x more in time-crunched games—not from ignorance, but from the brain’s threat-detection system overriding logic.

The hidden cost? The more you’ve mastered, the harder you fall. Expertise builds neural shortcuts, but under pressure, those shortcuts become traps. Pilots fixate on broken gauges ("confirmation bias" killed Air France 447). Traders double down on losing bets ("escalation of commitment" wiped out LT Capital). Your hard-won knowledge isn’t armor—it’s a liability, because pressure doesn’t expose gaps; it weaponizes what you think you know.

Still trust your instincts in a crisis?"
